title: The Anger That Refused to Be Fixed
dek: *You've tried every technique, read every book, practiced every breath. But what if the anger that won't leave is the one thing you shouldn't ask to go? Sometimes the most radical act of self-care is letting rage stay exactly as long as it needs to, unfixed and fiercely alive.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Some anger will not be fixed because it was never broken in the first place. The rage you've been trying to manage may be the only part of you still telling the truth. What you call dysfunction might be your body's most intelligent response to an impossible situation.

You've been told that all anger can be managed. That's the foundational lie of the anger-industrial complex, the one that keeps you cycling through workbooks and affirmations while the heat in your chest refuses to cool. You've downloaded apps. You've counted to ten. You've journaled your triggers until your hand cramped, and still, the anger returns. Not because you're doing it wrong, but because some anger isn't a problem to solve. It's information you haven't been willing to hear. ## When Fixing Becomes Another Form of Silencing The language of anger management carries an implicit promise: if you work hard enough, if you're disciplined enough, the anger will go away. But in our experience, the clients who struggle most aren't the ones who can't control their anger. They're the ones who've been controlling it so well for so long that they've forgotten what it was originally trying to say. Karla McLaren writes about anger as a boundary-setting emotion, one that arises specifically when our sense of self-preservation has been compromised. When you try to "fix" that anger without addressing the boundary violation underneath, you're essentially asking your nervous system to stop protecting you. You're negotiating with the smoke alarm instead of looking for the fire. The fixing impulse itself can become a form of spiritual bypassing. You tell yourself that evolved people don't stay angry. You reach for [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) not to understand your rage but to make it more palatable to others. You perform calm while your body keeps score, and the anger that refused to leave becomes shame about still being angry. Your jaw tightens when you smile. Your shoulders hike toward your ears in meetings. The headaches that arrive every Sunday night before the work week starts again, that knot in your stomach when you see certain names on your phone, the way your hands shake slightly when you have to be polite to someone who hurt you. These aren't symptoms of anger that needs managing. They're symptoms of anger that needs listening to. ## The Ones Who Never Got to Be Angry Over the years I've seen a particular pattern in people whose anger refuses resolution: they were the ones who weren't allowed to be angry as children. Not just discouraged from expressing it, but denied the right to feel it at all. Their anger had to stay underground, fermenting, because the cost of showing it was too high. You learned early that anger made you unlovable. That it proved you were difficult, ungrateful, too much. So you swallowed it. You turned it inward as depression, outward as people-pleasing, sideways as chronic anxiety. You became the reasonable one, the one who could always see both sides, the one who never made a fuss. And now, decades later, that banked anger has compound interest. Beverly Engel's work on shame and anger illuminates how deeply these two emotions intertwine. When you're shamed for your anger as a child, you don't just learn to hide the anger. You learn that the anger itself is evidence of your defectiveness. The emotion and the identity fuse. You're not someone who feels angry; you're an angry person, which in your family's lexicon meant a bad person. The anger that refuses to be fixed is often the anger that was never allowed to exist in the first place. It's not recalcitrant. It's waiting for you to finally give it permission to be. ## What Your Body Knows That Your Mind Keeps Denying There's a particular quality to the anger that won't leave: it lives in your body before it reaches your thoughts. You feel it as heat in your solar plexus, as tension in your hands, as a metallic taste in your mouth. It's somatic before it's cognitive, and that's precisely why cognitive interventions keep failing. You can't think your way out of something your body is trying to tell you. According to the APA's research summary on anger management, chronic anger often signals unresolved trauma or ongoing stressors that exceed our coping capacity. But what the research doesn't always capture is the intelligence of that chronicity. Your body is refusing to let go of the anger because the situation that caused it hasn't actually changed. You're still in the relationship. You're still at the job. You're still living in the family system that required your silence. The anger isn't broken. Your situation is. I watch clients contort themselves trying to be less angry about things that warrant rage. They try to reframe abuse as "difficult communication patterns." They try to find the lesson in betrayal. They explore [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) looking for something to take the edge off, and sometimes that's appropriate, sometimes [Banyan Botanicals Brahmi Oil (Sesame Base)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0D79SNVBL?tag=spankyspinola-20) on your temples and wrists can help you stay grounded enough to feel what you're feeling. But no adaptogen will make injustice feel okay. Your body is holding the anger because your body is the only part of you that hasn't learned to lie yet. ## The Anger That Protects You From Yourself Here's the part that gets uncomfortable: sometimes the anger that won't leave is protecting you from your own tendency to forgive too quickly, to understand too much, to give more chances than anyone deserves. It's the part of you that remembers every time you've betrayed yourself by being reasonable. Robert Augustus Masters distinguishes between what he calls "numbed-out anger" and "awakened anger." The first is anger that's been repressed so long it expresses itself as chronic resentment, passive aggression, or a generalized irritability that has no specific target. The second is anger that's been felt fully, that knows exactly what it's about, that carries both heat and clarity. The anger that refuses to be fixed might be in the process of waking up. It's done being reasonable. It's done being mature about situations that require a primal no. It's the part of you that's finally ready to stop negotiating with people who have no intention of meeting you halfway. And it feels dangerous because it is dangerous, to the version of yourself who survived by being accommodating. This anger doesn't want affirmations. It doesn't want to be soothed or understood or contextualized. It wants you to finally, finally act on what you've known for years. ## A Practice: Sitting With the Unfixable Here's something you can do right now, in less than five minutes, that won't fix your anger but might help you hear it. Find a private space. Set a timer for four minutes. Place one hand on your chest and one on your belly. Close your eyes if that feels safe. Now say out loud: "I'm angry, and I'm not going to fix it right now." Notice what happens in your body. Does your throat tighten? Does your chest open? Do you feel relief or panic or both? Keep breathing. Say it again: "I'm angry, and I'm not going to fix it right now." You're not feeding the anger. You're not indulging it. You're simply acknowledging that it exists and that it's allowed to exist without an immediate action plan for its removal. You're giving yourself permission to be where you are. When the timer goes off, thank your anger for whatever it's trying to protect. You don't have to know what that is yet. The thanking itself is the practice. ## When Anger Becomes Your Teacher Instead of Your Problem Across the articles we've published on this site, one theme surfaces repeatedly: the emotions we're most afraid of often carry the information we most need. Your anger knows things your politeness has been hiding. It knows which relationships are extractive. It knows which situations are untenable. It knows exactly how much you've been betraying your own needs in service of keeping the peace. What if you stopped trying to make it go away and started asking what it's trying to teach you? This doesn't mean acting on every angry impulse. It doesn't mean you get to be cruel or destructive. It means you start treating your anger as a source of data rather than a problem to fix. You start noticing what specifically triggers it. Not so you can avoid those triggers, but so you can understand what boundaries are being violated. Maybe you've been taking [Doctor's Best High Absorption Magnesium Glycinate, 240 Count](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000BD0RT0?tag=spankyspinola-20) to help with the physical tension, and that's good, your nervous system needs support. But the magnesium isn't going to tell you that you need to quit your job or leave your partner or stop talking to your mother. Your anger is trying to tell you that. The unfixable anger is often the anger that's asking you to change your life, not your emotional response to your life. ## The Grief Underneath the Rage Here's what nobody tells you about anger that won't leave: underneath it, there's almost always grief. Grief for the childhood you didn't get to have. Grief for the person you were before the betrayal. Grief for all the years you spent trying to be good enough for people who were never going to love you the way you needed. The anger is the lid on the grief. It's easier to be furious than heartbroken. Fury feels powerful. Heartbreak feels like dissolution. But the anger that refuses to be fixed is often anger that's guarding a wound so old and so deep that you've been afraid to look at it directly. It's anger that says: if I let myself feel how much this hurt, I won't survive it. So the anger stays, sentinel and shield, keeping you at a manageable distance from the pain underneath. Sometimes the work isn't managing the anger. It's finally, carefully, letting yourself cry. You might explore [the assessments hub](/assessments) to better understand your anger patterns, and that can be useful. [Bach Original Flower Remedies, Oak - Restore Endurance - Natural Homeopathic Flo](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005P0UPGY?tag=spankyspinola-20) might help when you need support for that particular kind of exhaustion that comes from holding on too long. But eventually, you have to ask: what am I holding onto, and what am I holding it for? ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I know if my anger is the "unfixable" kind or if I'm just avoiding doing the work? The unfixable kind has specificity. It knows exactly what it's about. It doesn't generalize or displace. It's anger at a particular person, situation, or pattern, and it remains consistent even when you're otherwise in a good mood. Avoidance-anger is vague, diffuse, and often accompanied by a sense that you should be over it by now. The unfixable kind doesn't think it should be over anything. It's still here because the situation is still unresolved. ### What if my anger is hurting my relationships? Then the question becomes: are those relationships worth preserving at the cost of your integrity? Not all relationships survive you starting to tell the truth. Some relationships were only functional because you were willing to be small and quiet and accommodating. Your anger might be telling you that those relationships need to end or radically transform. That's painful, but pain isn't the same as wrongness. ### Can anger ever actually be resolved, or am I stuck with it forever? Anger resolves when the need it's signaling gets met or when you genuinely accept that the need will never be met and grieve accordingly. Neither of those is a quick process. The anger that stays for years often stays because you keep hoping the situation will change without you having to do anything differently. When you finally act on what the anger is telling you, when you set the boundary or leave the situation or stop waiting for the apology that's never coming, the anger often dissolves on its own. Not because you fixed it, but because it completed its job. ## Living With the Fire There's a version of healing that looks like serenity, like you've transcended all your difficult emotions and arrived at some plateau of permanent peace. That's a fantasy, and probably a boring one. The real version of healing often looks like being angry about the right things and no longer apologizing for it. The anger that refused to be fixed might be the anger that's finally telling you the truth. It might be the part of you that's done performing wellness while your actual life remains intolerable. It might be your integrity, inconveniently located in your rage. You don't have to make friends with it. You don't have to love it or celebrate it. You just have to stop treating it like evidence that you're failing at being human. You're not failing. You're awake. And awake people get angry about things that deserve anger, and sometimes that anger doesn't resolve on a timeline that makes anyone comfortable. Maybe the question isn't how to fix the anger. Maybe the question is what life you'd have to build for the anger to finally feel heard. What boundaries you'd have to set. What situations you'd have to leave. What truths you'd have to speak. The anger isn't asking you to be perfect. It's asking you to be honest, and honesty is almost always the harder path. But it's the only one that leads anywhere real. For more perspectives on working with difficult emotions, [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers additional contemplative practices and seasonal wisdom. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on the anger that refused to be fixed, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
